narration.default: Real users type unexpected things. Real maths sometimes divides by zero. Until now, any error has crashed your program with a red wall of text. Today you'll learn try and except — Python's way of catching errors so the program can recover and keep running. You'll also meet finally, a block that ALWAYS runs, error or not.

Type:

try:
    a = int(input("Enter number: "))
    b = int(input("Enter another number: "))
    
    print(a / b)

try is standard Python — it tells Python "the indented lines below MIGHT fail; if they do, don't crash, jump to the except block I'll write next". The risky bits here are int(input(...)) (kid types non-digits?) and a / b (kid types 0 for b?).

Type:

except:
    print("Error occurred")

If any line inside the try fails, Python jumps straight here — skipping the rest of the try block — and runs the except block. Then the program keeps going, no crash. The except is your safety net.

Type:

finally:
    print("Done")

finally is also standard Python — it runs at the end whether or not there was an error. Useful for cleanup steps: closing files, hanging up network connections, saving progress. Even if the try crashes AND the except runs, finally still runs after.

Click Run. Type 10 for the first number, 2 for the second. The terminal prints 5.0 (Python's division gives a decimal) and Done. The try block ran cleanly, the except was skipped, and finally still ran at the end.

Run again. Type 10, then 0. The terminal prints Error occurred (the except caught the divide-by-zero) and Done (finally still ran). Without try/except, Python would have crashed loudly. With it, the program handles the problem gracefully and keeps moving.

Run once more. Type hello when it asks for a number. int can't convert that — error. Same except catches it, same Error occurred, same Done. One try/except handles many different kinds of failure, all in one neat block.

Use try/except whenever the line could fail because of OUTSIDE input — kid's text, files that might not exist, network calls that might time out. Don't use it to hide bugs in YOUR own code — those should be fixed, not silenced. Wrap the int(input(...)) line in any earlier game with try/except and the program stops crashing when the kid types nonsense — that one change makes any program much friendlier.
